topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot clattered open, and there it was: a thick, cream-colored card, completely blank save for the embossed script at the top. A birthday card. My jaw tightened so hard I thought my molars might crack. I snatched it up, the paper feeling flimsy and insulting in my hand. Who sends an empty gesture? I scanned the street, my eyes narrowing as I looked for the sender, for the smug face behind this deliberate void. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck. I crumpled the card once, twice, the sound a sharp, tearing noise in the quiet apartment. I threw it toward the bin, the motion jerky and violent. This was a deliberate slight, a performance of indifference wrapped in stationery. I needed to know who thought they could just drop this monument to apathy in my mailbox.

[story 2]

I stared at the card under the harsh glare of the kitchen light. Unsigned. Just a card, waiting to be filled with some saccharine platitude. A tight knot formed in my chest, constricting my breath. Every nerve ending felt hyper-alert, buzzing with a sudden, sharp hostility. I picked it up, turning it over and over, my thumb pressing into the thick cardstock until the fibers protested. It wasn't just an empty card; it felt like a deliberate snub, a silent declaration that I was invisible to someone who thought they had the right to send me something. I slammed the card onto the counter, the impact rattling a nearby glass. My voice, when I finally spoke, was dangerously low, laced with a brittle edge. "Who in the hell thinks they can just leave this?"

[story 3]

The weight of the envelope felt wrong in my hand. It was a birthday card, yet it held absolutely nothing. A slow, simmering heat began to rise from my core, making my palms slick. I stood motionless by the front door, my knuckles white where I gripped the mail. This wasn't forgetfulness; this was calculated disrespect. Someone had taken the time to mail something, only to deliver nothing but a void. My gaze swept the porch, searching for any familiar shape, any hint of recognition. When nothing materialized, the tension in my shoulders spiked, pulling my spine rigid. I let out a sharp, involuntary exhale, the sound ragged. I didn't just want to destroy the card; I wanted to tear the entire system that allowed such meaningless acts to exist apart. I marched back inside, my steps heavy and purposeful, and hurled the offending paper against the wall with a force that made the plaster vibrate.
